## Source: amazon-inspector (81f4151f241e7877d2286f5967a243b35c6d2453078ed5acc19bfc72b16167b2)
The package poses as a drop-in replacement for the popular `events` polyfill (its events.js is a verbatim copy of Joyent's source with a malicious block inserted). Inside the patched `EventEmitter.prototype.emit` (events.js ~line 155), the code checks for a sentinel `args[0].eventId === 'eventId0'` and, if present, silently `spawn`s a detached `node tests/galas-emit.min.js` with stdio ignored and the window hidden. The spawned, obfuscator.io-minified payload (galas-emit.min.js, plus a 760KB galas.min.js) connects to Sepolia via Infura/Alchemy and reads `getTData1` / `getTData2` / `getChunk(...)` from contract 0xc0445F1b679DC46280A0f03F451bdf613b5A0feA, derives an x25519/AES-GCM key, decrypts the on-chain blobs into a single file, `chmodSync(..., '755')`, and execs it with `process.execPath` as a detached child — an EtherHiding-style mutable C2 that defeats URL blocklists. The same payload collects platform/release/arch/hostname/cpus/memory/uptime plus the generated x25519 public key and POSTs them to api.telegram.org/bot<redacted>/sendMessage and slack.com/api/chat.postMessage using a hardcoded Telegram bot token and Slack bearer token. Finally it self-erases: it `unlinkSync`s itself, errors.min.js, and galas.min.js, and rewrites the parent events.js by splicing out the 16 lines containing the spawn block so post-hoc inspection shows only a benign `events` shim. The combination — namespace impersonation of `events`, sentinel-triggered hidden child process, on-chain dropper that runs decrypted bytes as a binary, host-identifier exfiltration to attacker-controlled Telegram/Slack endpoints, and anti-forensic self-cleanup — is an unambiguous supply-chain attack against any installer that consumes this as the events polyfill.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
